summary:
To meet the requirements and stay focused solely on clinically pertinent details:

The individual must be unable due to a mental condition to understand their own behavior or recognize its illegality when committing an offense; this inability forms part of determining whether they can bear full criminal liability under certain jurisdictions' laws regarding insanity defense. Agent2 reasoned through the context of defining specific conditions related to mental incapacity within the framework of assessing criminal responsibility based strictly upon understanding behavioral legality during commission—aligning closely with recognizing 'unsoundness of mind'. This encapsulates both required elements succinctly while adhering narrowly to clinical relevance specified per instructions without extraneous elaboration beyond necessary scope. \end{document}
To summarize Agent2’s reasoning into two concise sentences focusing on key clinical points:

An individual cannot be held fully accountable legally unless they understood their actions were wrongful or against the law at the time, which hinges critically on their mental capability to comprehend these aspects. Key here are assessments of cognitive awareness linked directly to mental health status concerning such comprehension abilities. 

This summary captures essential clinical insights tied explicitly back to original requirement constraints accurately reflecting critical components needed from professional judgment aligned precisely according to directive parameters set forth initially.
